question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker, under a sell offer (offer.buy=false) where buyerPrice is near WAD, trigger a rounding mismatch between TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits (which uses mulDivDown(WAD, buyerPrice)) and Midnight.take (which uses mulDivUp(buyerPrice, WAD) for sell offers), such that resBuyerAssets > targetFilledBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets for the last iteration, causing filledBuyerAssets to exceed targetFilledBuyerAssets and the require(filledBuyerAssets == targetFilledBuyerAssets) to revert with OutOfOffers() even though sufficient offer liquidity exists, violating the invariant that a valid set of offers covering targetBuyerAssets must be fillable? Proof idea: fuzz (tick, referralFeePct, targetBuyerAssets) across all allowed LLTV tiers and assert that buyerAssetsToUnits(units) fed back through take's mulDivUp never exceeds the remaining target by more than 1 wei.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker, under a buy offer (offer.buy=true) where buyerAssetsToUnits uses mulDivUp(WAD, buyerPrice) and take uses mulDivDown(buyerPrice, WAD), cause resBuyerAssets < targetFilledBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets for the last iteration, so filledBuyerAssets never reaches targetFilledBuyerAssets and the require reverts with OutOfOffers(), permanently blocking the taker from executing a valid bundle even when the offer has sufficient remaining capacity, violating the invariant that a correctly sized take must be completable? Proof idea: unit test with buyerPrice = k * PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP for small k and targetBuyerAssets not divisible by buyerPrice, assert round-trip mulDivUp then mulDivDown never undershoots.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker, under a sell offer (offer.buy=false) where sellerAssetsToUnits uses mulDivDown(WAD, sellerPrice) and take uses mulDivUp(sellerPrice, WAD), cause resSeller Assets to exceed targetFilledSellerAssets - filledSellerAssets for the last iteration, so filledSellerAssets overshoots targetFilledSellerAssets, the loop exits early, and require(filledSellerAssets == targetFilledSellerAssets) reverts with OutOfOffers(), blocking a valid borrow bundle, violating the invariant that a correctly sized sell bundle must complete? Proof idea: fuzz (tick, targetSellerAssets) with sellerPrice near PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP boundaries and assert sellerAssetsToUnits round-trip through take never overshoots by more than 1 wei.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker, under a buy offer (offer.buy=true) where sellerAssetsToUnits uses mulDivUp(WAD, sellerPrice) and take uses mulDivDown(sellerPrice, WAD), cause resSeller Assets to undershoot targetFilledSellerAssets - filledSellerAssets, so filledSellerAssets never reaches targetFilledSellerAssets and the require reverts with OutOfOffers(), even though the offer has sufficient capacity, violating the invariant that a valid sell bundle must be completable? Proof idea: unit test with sellerPrice = 1e12 (minimum PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP) and targetSellerAssets = 1, assert round-trip never undershoots.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker craft a sequence where TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits or sellerAssetsToUnits returns a units value that, when fed to Midnight.take, produces a buyerAssets or sellerAssets that differs from the target by exactly 1 wei due to PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP quantization in tickToPrice, causing the exact-equality require to always revert for that (tick, targetAssets) pair, effectively making certain offer ticks permanently unusable through the bundler? Proof idea: enumerate all ticks at DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=4 and all targetAssets in [1, 1e6] and assert no (tick, targetAssets) pair causes permanent OutOfOffers.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker set maxBuyerAssets to exactly the expected filledBuyerAssets without budgeting for the referral fee, causing the refund computation maxBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets - referralFeeAssets to underflow and revert, even though the takes themselves succeeded and filledUnits == targetUnits, violating the invariant that a successful fill must always produce a valid refund? Proof idea: fuzz (referralFeePct, filledBuyerAssets) with maxBuyerAssets = filledBuyerAssets and assert underflow occurs for any referralFeePct > 0.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker with referralFeePct near WAD-1 cause referralFeeAssets = filledBuyerAssets.mulDivDown(WAD-1, 1) to overflow uint256 or produce a value so large that maxBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets - referralFeeAssets underflows, reverting after all takes have already mutated Midnight state (consumed amounts, positions), violating the invariant that a bundler revert must not leave Midnight state partially updated? Proof idea: fuzz referralFeePct in [WAD/2, WAD-1] and assert the refund arithmetic never underflows for any filledBuyerAssets <= maxBuyerAssets.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker set minSellerAssets to a value that is achievable only if referralFeeAssets rounds down to zero (referralFeePct * filledSellerAssets < WAD), causing the check filledSellerAssets - referralFeeAssets >= minSellerAssets to pass when referralFeePct > 0 but fail when referralFeePct rounds up, creating an inconsistency between the documented fee formula and the actual check, violating the invariant that the receiver always gets at least minSellerAssets after fees? Proof idea: fuzz (referralFeePct, filledSellerAssets) near the rounding boundary and assert the check is monotone in referralFeePct.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker observe that the bundler sends exactly targetSellerAssets to receiver regardless of filledSellerAssets, and that referralFeeAssets = targetSellerAssets.mulDivDown(referralFeePct, WAD - referralFeePct) is computed with mulDivDown, so targetSellerAssets + referralFeeAssets < targetFilledSellerAssets by up to 1 wei due to rounding, leaving 1 wei of loan token permanently stuck in the bundler after a successful call, violating the invariant that the bundler must hold zero loan token balance after each call? Proof idea: fuzz (targetSellerAssets, referralFeePct) and assert bundler loan token balance is zero after every successful supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker observe that referralFeeAssets = targetBuyerAssets.mulDivDown(referralFeePct, WAD) and targetFilledBuyerAssets = targetBuyerAssets - referralFeeAssets, so targetFilledBuyerAssets + referralFeeAssets <= targetBuyerAssets with equality only when WAD divides targetBuyerAssets * referralFeePct, meaning up to 1 wei of loan token is permanently stuck in the bundler after a successful call, violating the invariant that the bundler must hold zero loan token balance after each call? Proof idea: fuzz (targetBuyerAssets, referralFeePct) and assert bundler loan token balance is zero post-call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged maker set offer.callback to a malicious ISellCallback contract that, during the onSell callback inside Midnight.take, calls back into buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with the same taker authorization, causing the bundler to execute a second round of takes and collateral withdrawals before the outer call's filledUnits and filledBuyerAssets are updated, allowing the taker to receive double the collateral or double the credit units while paying only once, violating the invariant that each bundle call must result in exactly one fill of targetUnits? Proof idea: implement a malicious ISellCallback that reenters the bundler and assert filledUnits and collateral balance invariants hold across both calls.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged maker set offer.callback to a malicious IBuyCallback that, during onBuy inside Midnight.take, calls back into supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget with the same taker, causing the bundler to supply additional collateral and execute more takes before the outer call's filledUnits and filledSellerAssets are updated, allowing the taker to borrow more than intended while the outer call's minSellerAssets check passes on stale filledSellerAssets, violating the invariant that the taker receives exactly the assets corresponding to targetUnits? Proof idea: implement a malicious IBuyCallback that reenters the bundler and assert filledSellerAssets and debt invariants.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged maker set offer.callback to a contract that, during the callback inside Midnight.take, calls Midnight.setConsumed(offer.group, type(uint256).max, offer.maker) to cancel all remaining offers in the group, then returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS, causing subsequent iterations of the bundler's loop to see consumableUnits = 0 for all remaining offers, so unitsToTake = 0 for all remaining takes, no progress is made, and OutOfOffers() reverts after the callback-modified take already mutated state, violating the invariant that a successful partial fill must not leave the taker worse off than before the bundle call? Proof idea: implement a malicious callback that cancels the group and assert the revert undoes all state changes.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged maker set offer.callback to a contract that, during onSell, calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral on behalf of the taker (if the maker is authorized by the taker), draining the taker's collateral before the bundler's collateralWithdrawals loop executes, causing the bundler's withdrawCollateral calls to revert with UnhealthyBorrower, reverting the entire bundle after the takes have already been executed, violating the invariant that a successful take must not be undone by a subsequent collateral withdrawal failure? Proof idea: implement a malicious callback that drains taker collateral and assert the bundle either completes atomically or reverts entirely.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget / buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker call any bundle function with takes.length == 0, causing an array out-of-bounds access on takes[0].offer.market.loanToken before any authorization or parameter check, reverting with a generic EVM error instead of a meaningful revert, and confirm that no assets are lost or state is mutated in this case? Proof idea: unit test all five bundle functions with takes = [] and assert clean revert with no state mutation.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker pass a takes array where takes[0].offer.market is a valid market but takes[1].offer.market has the same toId but a different struct (e.g., different rcfThreshold or enterGate), causing the InconsistentMarket check to pass (same id) but the collateralWithdrawals loop to use takes[0].offer.market which may have different collateralParams than what was actually used in takes[1], allowing collateral to be withdrawn from a market with different oracle or LLTV parameters than the one used for the take, violating the invariant that collateral withdrawals must use the same market as the executed takes? Proof idea: create two market structs with same id but different rcfThreshold and assert collateralWithdrawals uses the correct market.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker set receiver = address(0), causing SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(loanToken, address(0), filledSellerAssets - referralFeeAssets) to burn the taker's loan token proceeds after a successful borrow, so the taker incurs debt and supplies collateral but receives zero loan tokens, violating the invariant that a successful sell bundle must deliver at least minSellerAssets to a non-zero receiver? Proof idea: unit test with receiver = address(0) and assert the function either reverts or the taker receives zero tokens while debt is created.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker set collateralReceiver = address(0), causing Midnight.withdrawCollateral to send seized collateral to address(0), burning the taker's collateral after a successful buy bundle, violating the invariant that a successful collateral withdrawal must deliver assets to a non-zero receiver? Proof idea: unit test with collateralReceiver = address(0) and assert collateral is burned.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker set receiver = address(this) (the bundler itself), causing the seller assets to be sent to the bundler with no withdrawal mechanism, permanently locking the taker's loan token proceeds in the bundler contract, violating the invariant that the bundler must hold zero loan token balance after each call? Proof idea: unit test with receiver = address(bundler) and assert loan token balance of bundler is non-zero post-call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker pass a referralFeeRecipient = address(0) with referralFeePct > 0, causing SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(loanToken, address(0), referralFeeAssets) to burn the referral fee tokens, and confirm whether this constitutes a loss of protocol or user funds beyond what is documented? Proof idea: unit test with referralFeeRecipient = address(0) and referralFeePct = 1e15 and assert referral fee tokens are burned.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that touchMarket is called once at the start of the bundle but settlementFee is computed inside each Midnight.take call using the current block.timestamp, so if the fee setter changes the settlement fee between the bundler's touchMarket call and the take loop (e.g., via a front-running transaction), the taker pays a different buyerAssets than expected, violating the invariant that the taker's cost must be bounded by maxBuyerAssets as computed at bundle submission time? Proof idea: simulate a fee change between touchMarket and take and assert the taker's actual cost matches the expected cost.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker observe that TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits reads settlementFee at the time of the call, but Midnight.take recomputes settlementFee at execution time, so if the fee setter increases the settlement fee between the two calls (e.g., in the same block via a higher-priority transaction), buyerPrice in take exceeds buyerPrice in buyerAssetsToUnits, causing take to consume more buyerAssets than computed, so filledBuyerAssets > targetFilledBuyerAssets and the require reverts with OutOfOffers(), blocking the bundle? Proof idea: fuzz settlement fee changes between buyerAssetsToUnits and take and assert the require never reverts due to fee changes alone.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker observe that if block.timestamp crosses a settlement fee breakpoint (e.g., from 7 days to 1 day before maturity) between the bundler's TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits call and the actual Midnight.take call, the settlement fee changes discontinuously, causing buyerPrice to jump, so the computed unitsToTake produces a different buyerAssets in take than expected, violating the invariant that the taker pays exactly targetBuyerAssets? Proof idea: warp block.timestamp to each fee breakpoint boundary and assert the bundle completes correctly.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged taker who has authorized both the bundler and a malicious msg.sender on Midnight have the malicious msg.sender call buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with taker = victim, collateralWithdrawals draining all of the victim's collateral to collateralReceiver = attacker, after the takes have been executed on behalf of the victim, violating the invariant that collateral withdrawals must only be initiated by the taker or their explicitly intended delegate? Proof idea: implement a test where attacker is authorized by victim and calls the bundle with malicious collateralWithdrawals and assert collateral is drained.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IMidnightBundles.sol'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged msg.sender who is authorized by taker on Midnight call supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget with taker = victim and receiver = attacker, causing the victim's collateral to be supplied and debt to be created while the loan token proceeds are sent to the attacker, violating the invariant that the taker's assets must only be transferred to addresses the taker has explicitly designated? Proof idea: implement a test where attacker is authorized by victim and calls the bundle with receiver = attacker and assert loan tokens are sent to attacker.
